title: Ensure that the controller manager pod specification file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive

input_row.title: Ensure that the controller manager pod specification file permissions are set to 600 or more restrictive
input_row.description: Ensure that the controller manager pod specification file has permissions of 600 or more restrictive.
input_row.rationale: The controller manager pod specification file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the Controller Manager on the master node. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system. Impact: None
input_row.audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml Verify that the permissions are 600 or more restrictive.
input_row.remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod 600 /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml Default Value: By default, the kube-controller-manager.yaml file has permissions of 640. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- This control requires verification of the file permissions of the kube-controller-manager pod specification file on the host filesystem (e.g., /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml).
- Kubernetes APIs and kubectl do not expose host file permissions; they only expose pod specs, not the underlying file system metadata.
- The audit procedure explicitly instructs to use OS-level commands (e.g., stat) on the control plane node.
- Therefore, this check cannot be performed via Kubernetes APIs or kubectl and requires direct host/OS access.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into the Kubernetes control plane node where the kube-controller-manager runs.
2. Locate the kube-controller-manager pod specification file (commonly at /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml).
3. Run the following command to check the file permissions:
   ```
   stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-controller-manager.yaml
   ```
4. Verify that the output is 600 or a more restrictive value (e.g., 400). The file should not be group- or world-readable/writable.
5. If the permissions are more permissive than 600 (e.g., 640, 644, 660, 666, etc.), the control is NOT compliant.
6. If the permissions are 600 or more restrictive, the control is compliant.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.11.1 PDF
